topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent hum of the shared workspace was the only constant in Elara’s world. She meticulously cross-referenced the schematics on her screen, her fingers hovering over the keyboard, a slight tremor running through her hand. It was the third time this week she’d found an exact match for the preliminary design documents she’d been wrestling with. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She glanced up, catching a flicker of movement across the aisle—a silhouette leaning over a workbench, tracing lines on a blueprint that bore an uncanny resemblance to her own. Elara pulled her gaze back down, forcing a steadying breath. She needed to confirm. She moved slowly, deliberately, towards the shared resource shelf, her steps measured, every sound amplified in the quiet tension of the room. When she finally approached, the other person looked up, and for a split second, their eyes met hers—a shared, unspoken acknowledgment of the uncanny duplication.

[story 2]

Mark kept wiping the condensation from his glasses, the rhythmic motion a small attempt to anchor himself against the rising tide of unease. Every time he reached for the data logs, a phantom sensation of being watched prickled the back of his neck. He reviewed the iteration history for the software build, his jaw tightening as he noticed the timestamp alignment—identical. It wasn't just similar; it was a mirror image. He shifted in his chair, the squeak of the worn leather protesting the movement, and quickly checked the door, a reflexive sweep of his eyes across the empty corridor. He felt a need to establish boundaries, to map the perimeter of this unsettling similarity. When he finally turned to face the source of the anomaly, the other individual was already packing away tools, their movements precise and almost unnervingly familiar. Mark leaned against the desk, his posture rigid, his voice coming out unexpectedly tight. "Are you... working on the same thing?"

[story 3]

For Lena, the air in the lab felt thick, pressurized. She kept glancing over her shoulder, cataloging every sound, every shadow that shifted in the periphery of her vision. The blueprints laid out on the central table were an infuriating echo of her own latest drafts, down to the marginal notations. A flush crept up her neck, and she consciously forced her breathing to remain even, refusing to let any spike of anxiety show in her posture. She felt compelled to verify the impossible. Slowly, she rose, moving with an almost unnatural economy of motion toward the adjoining station. As she neared, she saw the other person hunched over the same design, their concentration absolute. Lena stopped just short of invading their space, her gaze fixed on the shared screen. A low, controlled tone escaped her lips, laced with a sharp edge. "This isn't coincidence," she stated, the words clipped and unwavering, her hands resting flat on the table surface, ready for whatever came next.
